metadata.title: Free Image Color Extractor – Palette From Any Photo
metadata.description: Upload a photo or logo and extract up to 12 dominant colors with HEX and RGB codes. Perfect for UI, branding and social media. No signup, no limits.

hero.title: Image Color Extractor – Get a Palette From Any Photo
hero.description: Upload a photo, logo, or graphic, and the tool will extract up to 12 dominant colors with HEX and RGB codes. Analysis happens locally in your browser.

software.name: Image color extractor — get a palette from any photo
alternateName: Extract color palette from a photo or logo
software.description: Free image color extractor. Upload a photo, logo, or graphic and get a palette of up to 12 dominant colors with HEX and RGB codes. Processing happens in the browser - files are not sent to a server.
featureList: Automatic extraction of up to 12 dominant colors
Analysis of photos, logos, and vector graphics
Color codes in HEX and RGB format
Support for PNG, JPG, and SVG files
Ignores transparent pixels in PNG files
Local processing in the browser - image is not sent to a server

howTo.name: How to extract colors from a photo or image
howTo.description: How to extract dominant colors from a photo or logo. Upload an image in PNG, JPG, or SVG format, and the tool will generate a palette of up to 12 colors with HEX codes.

steps.name: Upload an image
steps.text: Drag a file onto the designated area or select an image from your device. Supported formats are PNG, JPG, and SVG.

steps.name: Wait for analysis
steps.text: The tool will automatically analyze the image and extract dominant colors.

steps.name: Copy colors
steps.text: Next to each color you will find a copy button - the HEX code is copied to your system clipboard.

contentBlocks.title: Why extract colors from an image?
contentBlocks.html:
Every photo, logo, or graphic contains colors that can serve as a ready-made color palette. The extractor analyzes the image and shows which colors dominate - along with HEX codes and RGB values ready to paste into your project.

In practice, this means that instead of manually sampling colors in a graphics application (pixel by pixel), you upload one image and get an organized list of up to 12 colors. This is useful when selecting colors for a website, creating consistent social media graphics, or building a visual identity from existing material.

All analysis happens locally in the browser - the image is not sent to any server.

contentBlocks.title: How to use the image color extractor
contentBlocks.description: The whole process takes just a few seconds:

items.title: 1. Upload an image
items.description: Drag a file onto the designated area or select it from your device. Supported formats are PNG, JPG, and SVG.

items.title: 2. Wait for analysis
items.description: The tool will automatically analyze the image and extract up to 12 dominant colors.

items.title: 3. Copy the colors you need
items.description: Next to each color there is a copy button - the HEX or RGB code goes to the clipboard and can be pasted directly into your project.

Example colors extracted from a nature image
contentBlocks.html:
After uploading an image, the extractor displays a list of dominant colors sorted from most to least prominent. Each color shows its HEX code and RGB value - ready to paste into CSS, Figma, or any graphics application.

The number of extracted colors depends on the image content. A landscape photo will produce a richer palette (8–12 colors), while a simple two-color logo will yield correspondingly fewer items.

contentBlocks.title: Which images give the best results in the color extractor?
contentBlocks.description: The quality of the extracted palette depends on the type of uploaded image:

items.title: Logos and graphics with a limited palette
items.description: Images with a few clearly separated colors give the most precise results - the extractor accurately identifies each color.

items.title: Photos with a clear subject
items.description: Product, interior, or landscape photos also produce useful palettes, but they contain more shades - including shadow colors and light reflections.

items.title: PNG files with transparent backgrounds
items.description: Transparent pixels are automatically skipped during analysis. If you want to extract colors only from the object (e.g., a logo), use a PNG file without a background.

items.title: Images without a large solid-color background
items.description: When the background takes up most of the image area, its color dominates the results. Before uploading, consider cropping the image to the area of interest.

contentBlocks.title: How does image color extraction work?
contentBlocks.description: After uploading a file, the tool performs several steps in the background - all analysis happens locally in the browser:

items.title: Image scaling
items.description: The image is resized to about 240 pixels wide. This speeds up analysis even for very large photos, without affecting color detection accuracy.

items.title: Grouping similar colors
items.description: Each pixel is analyzed, and similar shades are grouped together. Two nearly identical blues become one color in the palette.

items.title: Selecting dominant colors
items.description: The algorithm picks colors that cover the largest area of the image. The result is a list of up to 12 colors sorted from most to least prominent.

items.title: Skipping transparent pixels
items.description: In PNG files with transparent backgrounds, those areas are not included in the analysis - the extractor examines only visible colors. The entire process typically takes under a second.

items.title: Extractor returned fewer than 12 colors
items.description: The number of extracted colors depends on the image content. A simple two-color logo will give 2–3 results - this is correct behavior. The tool does not add artificial colors; it only extracts those that actually appear in the image.

items.title: Unexpected colors appeared in the palette
items.description: These may be shadow, gradient, or light reflection colors - pixels in those areas have different colors than the object visible at first glance. Using an image with more uniform colors or cropping dark areas will improve results.

items.title: Background color dominates the results
items.description: When the background covers more area than the object itself, its color will appear first on the list. Solution: use a PNG file with a transparent background or crop the image so the object takes up more of the frame.

items.title: File is not accepted by the extractor
items.description: The tool only supports PNG, JPG, and SVG formats. Files in other formats (GIF, TIFF, HEIC, PDF) require prior conversion.

contentBlocks.title: What makes this image color extractor special?

items.title: Local analysis - the image never leaves your computer
items.description: All color extraction happens in the browser. The image is not sent to any server, and after closing the page, data is removed from memory.

items.title: Up to 12 dominant colors from one image
items.description: The tool extracts up to 12 colors sorted from most prominent - enough to build a complete project color palette.

items.title: Three popular image formats
items.description: Supported formats are PNG, JPG, and SVG. PNG files with transparent backgrounds give the best results because transparent pixels are automatically skipped.

items.title: Ready HEX and RGB codes to paste
items.description: Each extracted color comes with its HEX code (e.g., #2C5F2D) and RGB value. The code can be copied to the clipboard and pasted directly into CSS, Figma, or Photoshop.

contentBlocks.title: Where to use colors extracted from an image

items.title: Building visual identity
items.description: Upload a photo that captures the mood of the brand - a landscape, a restaurant interior, a product photo - and extract colors as a starting point for a branding palette.

items.title: Social media graphics
items.description: Extract colors from a product photo and use them as backgrounds or accents in graphics. Posts based on colors from the same source look consistent on the profile.

items.title: Matching colors to a website
items.description: Extract colors from the logo and use them as the website color palette - primary color, accent color, background shades. HEX codes paste straight into CSS.

items.title: Expanding an existing palette
items.description: An extracted base color can be used in the color palette generator to create a full set of harmonious colors based on color theory.

contentBlocks.title: Frequently asked questions about the image color extractor

items.question: Does the image color extractor require registration?
items.answer: No. The tool is completely free and requires no login or registration. The image is analyzed locally in the browser - it is not sent to any server. After closing the page, all data is removed.
items.answerSchemaText: No. The tool is free and does not require registration. The image is analyzed locally in the browser.

items.question: How is the color extractor different from a palette generator?
items.answer: The extractor pulls colors from an existing image - it analyzes a photo and shows which colors dominate in it. A color palette generator works the other way: you provide one base color, and the tool creates harmonious sets based on color theory. Both tools complement each other - you can extract a color from an image and then use it as a base in the palette generator.
items.answerSchemaText: The extractor pulls colors from an image. The generator creates palettes from a base color. Both tools complement each other.

items.question: How many colors does the extractor pull from one image?
items.answer: The tool extracts up to 12 dominant colors. The exact number depends on the image content - if a graphic has only two colors (e.g., a simple logo), the result will contain fewer items. Colors are sorted from most to least prominent.
items.answerSchemaText: Up to 12 dominant colors, sorted from the most to least prominent.

items.question: What image formats does the color extractor support?
items.answer: Supported formats are PNG, JPG (JPEG), and SVG. PNG files with transparent backgrounds give better results because transparent pixels are skipped during analysis - the tool focuses on the colors of the object itself.
items.answerSchemaText: PNG, JPG, and SVG. PNG with transparent background gives the best results.

items.question: In what format do I get the extracted color codes?
items.answer: Each color is displayed with its HEX code (e.g., #2C5F2D) and RGB value (e.g., rgb(44, 95, 45)). The code can be copied to the clipboard and pasted directly into CSS, Figma, Photoshop, or any other graphics application.
items.answerSchemaText: HEX (e.g., #2C5F2D) and RGB (e.g., rgb(44, 95, 45)). Copyable to clipboard.

items.question: Can I copy all palette colors at once?
items.answer: Currently the tool allows copying colors individually - next to each color there is a button that copies the HEX code to the clipboard. The copied code can be immediately pasted into Figma, Photoshop, CSS, or any other application.
items.answerSchemaText: Colors are copied individually via the copy button next to each color.
